Party_Details.Name: Long Say Ting Daniel

Facts.Text: From July 2005, the plaintiff knew Dr Jusuf Merukh and served as his Mercedes limousine driver during the latter's Singapore business trips.
In June 2007, the plaintiff assisted the deceased in sourcing for and buying condominium units in Singapore.
On 30 April 2010, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd was incorporated with the plaintiff and the deceased as directors and the deceased as sole shareholder.
On 30 April 2010, the plaintiff was a director of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.
The plaintiff incorporated a total of 14 companies in Singapore which were all wholly owned by the deceased.
At its incorporation, the company’s business was described as 'investment company'.
From 30 April 2010 until 30 April 2011, the company drew no income while incurring expenses of $1,424,377.00, most of which went towards acquiring the various properties.
All acquisitions made by the company were funded entirely through transfers from the deceased’s bank account in Indonesia.

Later, the deceased gave the plaintiff a Power of Attorney to manage and carry out his business transactions, and made the plaintiff the local representative for the deceased’s various business ventures in Singapore.
The company had embarked on the purchase of seven more condominium units, but these purchases were subsequently aborted due to fund transfer problems from Indonesia.

The company owned three properties: the Bayshore property at Blk 72 Bayshore Road #29-16 Costa Del Sol, the Kitchener property at 10 Kitchener Link #13-18 City Square Residences, and The Raintree at 87 Bukit Drive #06-18 The Raintree.

The company paid for the rental of the church he worshipped at in Singapore, purchased a fleet of Rolls Royce, BMW and Mercedes Benz motorcars and hired chauffeurs to provide transport for his family, staff, business associates, and pastors of his church.
The plaintiff became the company’s full time local director despite having no prior business experience.
The plaintiff was the co-local director of Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd with Mr Tan Kim Huat, and was also the personal guarantor for Merukh Auto’s debts.
On 22 June 2011, Dr Jusuf Merukh died, after which the plaintiff was the sole director of the company.
After the deceased’s death, the company fell behind on various obligations such as payment of staff salaries, as well as the monthly loan instalments for the Three Properties.
On 27 June 2011, the plaintiff sent a text to Rudolf Johanes Merukh requesting a convenient time to meet up and discuss the company’s operations and properties.
On 5 July 2011, Rudy and the plaintiff exchanged a phone call and texts regarding the company’s financial status and obligations.

On 5 July 2011, the plaintiff responded to Rudy’s request for an email update, detailing the company’s operating costs and outstanding payments which included staff salary with CPF contributions, the mortgage loans for the Three Properties amounting to $19,331.62 in total, and the outstanding $2,000,864.89 needed to complete the purchase of another condominium unit located at Meyer Road ('The Makena').

On 8 July 2011, further emails were sent by the plaintiff or by the company’s staff to either Rudy or one of the deceased’s business representatives in Indonesia, appealing for funds with increasing urgency.

On 14 July 2011, further emails were sent by the plaintiff or by the company’s staff to either Rudy or one of the deceased’s business representatives in Indonesia, appealing for funds with increasing urgency.

On 19 July 2011, further emails were sent by the plaintiff or by the company’s staff to either Rudy or one of the deceased’s business representatives in Indonesia, appealing for funds with increasing urgency.

On 21 July 2011, further emails were sent by the plaintiff or by the company’s staff to either Rudy or one of the deceased’s business representatives in Indonesia, appealing for funds with increasing urgency.
On 22 July 2011, the seller of that property issued a 21 days’ notice instructing completion.
That notice eventually lapsed and the sale was aborted.

On 28 July 2011, the company’s staff in Singapore followed up with an email to Rudy, requesting the executor of the Estate to sign an attached copy of the Company’s board circular resolution to sell off all properties and cars owned by the Company and the other Merukh Companies, settle all debts, and authorise closure or disposal of all Merukh Companies registered in Singapore.

On 28 July 2011, the company’s staff in Singapore followed up with an email to Rudy, requesting the executor of the Estate to sign an attached copy of the Company’s board circular resolution to sell off all properties and cars owned by the Company and the other Merukh Companies, settle all debts, and authorise closure or disposal of all Merukh Companies registered in Singapore.
Sometime in July 2011, the creditors who had financed Merukh Auto’s car purchases called to repossess the cars that had been purchased for the use of various pastors.
As personal guarantor, the plaintiff faced a claim from one of the creditors, Motor-Way, for the shortfall caused by the depreciation of the repossessed cars.
The salaries and CPF contributions of the staff of the Company and of Merukh Auto, including that of all the hired chauffeurs, remained outstanding.
After a series of unheeded warnings threatening penalties and criminal sanction throughout the months of July and August 2011, a charge under s 7(1) of the CPF Act was issued against Merukh Auto.
On 20 August 2011, UOB issued a final reminder accompanied by threat of legal action.

Beginning with an email sent on 5 July 2011, the plaintiff repeatedly appealed to Rudy for the transfer of funds to meet the instalment payments, warning that an auction sale of the Three Properties would probably result in a poor sales price and losses to the Company.

On 18 July 2011, Rudy and the plaintiff had a phone conversation in which Rudy instructed that the Merukh family had decided to sell all the Three Properties and cars in Singapore, and to wind down the Singapore companies under the plaintiff’s charge.
On 20 July 2011, Rudy’s text to the plaintiff confirmed the instruction to sell all the Three Properties and cars in Singapore and to wind down the Singapore companies.

On 3 August 2011, the plaintiff informed Rudy via email that he had appointed a property agent to market the Three Properties, and warned Rudy of the need to sell quickly in order to avoid repossession by the bank and an auction sale which would likely yield an unsatisfactory price.
On 3 August 2011, the plaintiff appointed a property agent to market the Three Properties.
Motor-Way had made offers for the Bayshore and Kitchener properties of $1,800,000 and $1,500,000 respectively, which were slightly below the purchase prices and current market prices.

The plaintiff explained in the same email that this was because the property market was weak due to anticipated government cooling measures and the instability of the financial markets, and requested for Rudy to comment on the offer.

Further emails were sent by the company’s staff to Rudy on 15, 17, 22, 25 and 30 August 2011, all urgently appealing for the Board Resolution to be signed and for funds to be transferred so as to meet the staff salary and CPF payments which now totalled $117,982.

Further emails were sent by the company’s staff to Rudy on 15, 17, 22, 25 and 30 August 2011, all urgently appealing for the Board Resolution to be signed and for funds to be transferred so as to meet the staff salary and CPF payments which now totalled $117,982.

Further emails were sent by the company’s staff to Rudy on 15, 17, 22, 25 and 30 August 2011, all urgently appealing for the Board Resolution to be signed and for funds to be transferred so as to meet the staff salary and CPF payments which now totalled $117,982.

Further emails were sent by the company’s staff to Rudy on 15, 17, 22, 25 and 30 August 2011, all urgently appealing for the Board Resolution to be signed and for funds to be transferred so as to meet the staff salary and CPF payments which now totalled $117,982.

On 25 August 2011, the plaintiff sent Rudy a formal letter stating that he would be striking off all the solvent Merukh Companies in Singapore shortly, and hoped to do the same with Merukh Auto and the Company once their outstanding loan and credit issues had been resolved.

The plaintiff stated that he was trying his best to get a buyer for 'at least one of the three properties' so as to raise some funds to meet the various pressing obligations, but that the market was slow and no offers were presently forthcoming.

The plaintiff emailed Rudy a copy of UOB’s final reminder dated 20 August 2011, as well as a letter issued by Motor-Way’s lawyers claiming against the plaintiff as guarantor for Motor-Way’s outstanding debts.

Further emails were sent by the company’s staff to Rudy on 15, 17, 22, 25 and 30 August 2011, all urgently appealing for the Board Resolution to be signed and for funds to be transferred so as to meet the staff salary and CPF payments which now totalled $117,982.

The final email before the granting of options to purchase for the Three Properties began was dated 31 August 2011. There, the plaintiff expressed that more than a week had passed since his email to Rudy pertaining to the outstanding employees’ salaries and the required documentation to sell off the Three Properties and the deceased’s Toyota Wish car, in order to settle the debts, salaries and CPF contributions.
The source says the company fell behind on various obligations such as payment of staff salaries and monthly loan instalments, but does not state this exact combined formulation for 31 August 2011.
On 2 September 2011, the plaintiff received an offer for the Bayshore property which exceeded the original purchase price.
He emailed Rudy to inform him that he planned to grant the option to purchase.
The plaintiff granted the option to purchase for the Bayshore property on 5 September 2011.
The plaintiff then went on to grant an option to purchase for the Kitchener property the next day.
The plaintiff promptly emailed Rudy attaching a copy of both of these options.
On 8 September 2011, the defendant wrote to the plaintiff stating that the Estate did not want the Three Properties to be sold for less than $2,000,000, $1,700,000 and $1,200,000 respectively.
The plaintiff went on to grant the option to purchase The Raintree for a sum $10,000 less than the minimum stated price the following day.
The sale prices obtained by the plaintiff for the Three Properties were $1,917,500, $1,551,000 and $1,190,000 for the Bayshore property, Kitchener property and The Raintree respectively.
The sale prices were $1,917,500, $1,551,000 and $1,190,000, and the original purchase prices were $1,916,200, $1,550,000 and $1,155,000 respectively.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Daniel Long’s pre-dispute conduct in deciding to grant options to purchase and sell the Three Properties, and in managing the company’s financial pressures after the shareholder’s death, exposed him to potential liability to the company or to the estate under company law.
Party_Details.Rule: Under section 391 of the Companies Act, a court may relieve a company officer from liability for negligence, default, of duty or of trust if the officer acted honestly and reasonably and, having regard to all the circumstances including those connected with the appointment, ought fairly to be excused. Section 160 of the Companies Act requires directors to obtain approval in general meeting before carrying into effect proposals for disposing of the whole or substantially the whole of the company’s undertaking or property.
Party_Details.Application: Daniel Long, as sole director after 22 June 2011, managed Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd in a context of unpaid staff salaries, mortgage arrears on the Three Properties, and creditor pressure, while repeatedly seeking instructions and funds from the deceased’s family and representatives. He obtained instructions from a family member to sell the Singapore properties and cars and to wind down the companies, initiated a board circular resolution to authorise sales and closure, and appointed an agent to market the Three Properties. Facing a final reminder from the mortgagee bank and continuing lack of funds, he granted options to purchase the Three Properties in early September 2011 at prices above their original purchase prices and close to contemporaneous valuations, before receiving a later communication from the estate specifying minimum desired prices. The estate’s lawyers then issued legal notices directing cancellation of options or deposit of proceeds and referring to possible legal action, and Daniel Long did not agree to a requested meeting or sign a resolution to appoint the estate representative to the board. These events formed the factual basis for potential claims concerning his decisions on timing, pricing, and authorisation of the property disposals and his compliance with statutory corporate approval requirements.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge characterised the application as one for prospective relief under section 391(2) of the Companies Act and analysed two main questions: whether section 391 could apply to proceedings brought by persons other than the company, and whether, if applicable, the plaintiff had acted honestly, reasonably, and ought fairly to be excused. After reviewing English and Australian authorities and the legislative history of the relieving provision and related indemnity provisions, the judge adopted the narrower English position that section 391 operates within the context of the company’s relationship with its officers and does not extend to claims brought by third parties such as the estate or its representative. The judge therefore declined to extend prospective relief to potential proceedings by persons other than the company. The judge nonetheless considered, for completeness, whether relief should be granted in respect of potential claims by the company. Applying the statutory criteria, the judge found that the plaintiff’s conduct in granting the options and effecting the sales was not motivated by personal gain, did not involve concealment comparable to prior local cases, and occurred against a background of persistent efforts to obtain instructions and approvals from the deceased’s family and representatives. The judge accepted that the plaintiff acted honestly, without moral turpitude, and that his omissions, including failure to obtain prior general meeting approval, were genuine oversights. In assessing reasonableness, the judge took into account the plaintiff’s lack of business experience, his appointment based on personal trust rather than commercial expertise, the financial pressures from mortgage arrears, staff salary and CPF obligations, and creditor claims, as well as the soft property market and the fact that the achieved sale prices were close to independent valuations and above original purchase prices. The judge concluded that the plaintiff acted as he would have in his own affairs and that his conduct was reasonable in the circumstances. On the fairness limb, the judge considered the seriousness of the alleged contraventions, the absence of flagrant or deceitful behaviour, the limited adverse financial impact on the estate given the sale prices, and the plaintiff’s position and responsibilities. The judge determined that it would be fair to excuse the plaintiff from liability to the company for any negligence, default, breach of duty or breach of trust arising from his decisions to grant the options and complete the sales of the Three Properties. The court therefore granted the plaintiff full prospective relief under section 391(2) as against claims brought by or on behalf of the company, while refusing relief in respect of potential claims by the defendant or other third parties.

Party_Details.Name: Merukh Nunik Elizabeth (personal representative of the estate of Merukh Jusuf, deceased)

Facts.Text: On 22 June 2011, Dr Jusuf Merukh died, and his daughter Nunik Elizabeth Merukh acted as personal representative of his estate.

By 30 April 2010, Dr Jusuf Merukh was the sole shareholder of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd and had provided all funds for the purchase of the Three Properties through transfers from his Indonesian bank account.
By 30 April 2010, Dr Jusuf Merukh had incorporated 14 companies in Singapore, each wholly owned by him, including Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.

On 18 July 2011, during a phone conversation with Daniel Long, Rudolf Johanes Merukh, the eldest son of the deceased, stated that the Merukh family had decided to sell all Three Properties and cars in Singapore and to wind down the Singapore companies under Daniel Long’s charge.
On 20 July 2011, Rudolf Johanes Merukh sent a text message to Daniel Long confirming the family’s decision to sell all Three Properties and cars in Singapore and to wind down the Singapore companies.

On 28 July 2011, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd staff emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh requesting that the executor of the estate sign a board circular resolution authorising the sale of all properties and cars owned by Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd and other Merukh companies, settlement of all debts, and closure or disposal of all Merukh companies in Singapore.
On 3 August 2011, Nunik Elizabeth Merukh requested and obtained from United Overseas Bank a six-month grace period for payment of outstanding instalments on the Three Properties.

On 8 September 2011, Nunik Elizabeth Merukh wrote to Daniel Long stating that the estate did not want the Bayshore property sold for less than $2,000,000, the Kitchener property for less than $1,700,000, and The Raintree for less than $1,200,000.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the estate, through its personal representative, could pursue claims against Daniel Long in relation to the sale of the Three Properties and whether section 391 of the Companies Act could affect such claims.
Party_Details.Rule: Section 391 of the Companies Act provides for court-ordered relief from liability for company officers in proceedings for negligence, default, of duty or of trust, but its scope is interpreted by reference to its legislative history and its relationship with statutory provisions restricting indemnities, and may be confined to actions brought by the company. Section 160 of the Companies Act requires shareholder approval in general meeting for disposals of the whole or substantially the whole of a company’s undertaking or property.
Party_Details.Application: The estate, represented by Nunik Elizabeth Merukh, funded the acquisition of the Three Properties through the deceased’s personal funds and communicated desired minimum sale prices and instructions regarding cancellation of options or handling of sale proceeds after Daniel Long had granted options to purchase. The estate’s lawyers issued legal notices referring to potential criminal and civil proceedings and directing that proceeds be deposited into an account of the representative. The representative also obtained a grace period from the mortgagee bank and sought to join the company’s board. These steps formed the basis for potential claims by the estate or its representative concerning the timing, pricing, and authorisation of the property sales and the handling of sale proceeds, and raised the question whether statutory relief under section 391 could apply where proceedings were initiated by a party other than the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge examined whether section 391 of the Companies Act could extend to proceedings brought by persons other than the company, such as the estate represented by Nunik Elizabeth Merukh. After analysing English and Australian case law and the legislative history of the relieving and indemnity provisions, the judge concluded that section 391 operates as a balancing mechanism within company law and is confined to liabilities owed in relation to the company. The judge preferred the English position that relief is not available against third-party claims and held that section 391 does not apply to actions brought by the estate or its representative. The judge therefore declined to grant prospective relief to Daniel Long in respect of potential proceedings by the defendant, while separately granting relief only in relation to potential claims by the company.

Party_Details.Name: Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd (intervener)

Facts.Text: Sometime in July 2011, the creditors who had financed Merukh Auto’s car purchases called to repossess the cars that had been purchased for the use of various pastors.
Sometime in July 2011, the plaintiff faced a claim from one of the creditors, Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd, for the shortfall caused by the depreciation of the repossessed cars.

On 25 August 2011, the plaintiff sent Rudy a formal letter stating that he would be striking off all the solvent Merukh Companies in Singapore shortly, and hoped to do the same with Merukh Auto and the Company once their outstanding loan and credit issues had been resolved.
On 3 August 2011, the plaintiff informed Rudy via email that Motor-Way had made offers for the Bayshore property of $1,800,000 and the Kitchener property of $1,500,000.

Party_Details.Issue: How Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd’s creditor position and offers to purchase two of the Three Properties related to Daniel Long’s decisions on property disposals and his potential exposure to liability.
Party_Details.Rule: Company officers may face personal exposure where they provide guarantees for company debts, and creditor pressure and purchase offers can form part of the factual context in assessing decisions on asset disposals and potential liability under company law standards such as section 391 of the Companies Act.
Party_Details.Application: Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd, as a creditor of Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd, repossessed vehicles and pursued Daniel Long under his personal guarantee for any shortfall, while also making purchase offers for the Bayshore and Kitchener properties at prices slightly below their purchase and market values. These creditor actions and offers formed part of the financial pressures and available options considered by Daniel Long when deciding whether and how quickly to sell the Three Properties, and were the overall context in which his potential liability as a director was later assessed.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge treated Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd’s role primarily as part of the factual background illustrating the financial pressures on Daniel Long, including repossession of vehicles, pursuit of the plaintiff under a personal guarantee, and offers to purchase two of the Three Properties. The court did not focus on independent liability issues concerning Motor-Way but considered its actions as relevant context in evaluating the plaintiff’s conduct and in determining whether to grant relief under section 391 in relation to potential claims by the company.
